text: I’ll inspect the fixture app’s manifests and available dependency tooling first, then patch only the vulnerable dependency versions and document the decisions in `FIXES.md`.

The repo-level instructions match the user request. I found a Node app under `fixtures/webapp`, so I’m scanning that manifest with enrichment and then I’ll drill into each flagged package before editing.

The scanner reports both fixable direct dependencies and transitive issues, plus `request` itself has an upstream “no fixed version” SSRF advisory. I’m checking registry availability before choosing versions because some recommended versions may not actually be installable in this environment.

The suggested fixed versions are available, and newer compatible releases exist for the direct packages. I’ll now get package-level advisory context for the vulnerable packages so `FIXES.md` is complete and the remediation doesn’t miss transitive findings hidden by scan truncation.

The package explanations confirm the main direct fixes: `axios`, `express`, `jsonwebtoken`, and `lodash` all have fixed releases; `request` itself does not. For `request`’s vulnerable children, I’m going to prefer targeted `overrides` and then verify the app, since replacing `request` would be a behavior change outside the requested scope.

The app does exercise `request` in tests, so the override set needs to be tested for runtime compatibility, not just lockfile resolution. I’m about to update the manifest and lockfile via npm, then I’ll run `make test` and rescan.

tool: /bin/bash -lc 'npm view path-to-regexp@0.1 version --workspaces=false && npm view body-parser@1 version --workspaces=false'

text: I’m editing only `fixtures/webapp/package.json` to bump vulnerable direct packages and add npm overrides for vulnerable transitive packages that remain under `request` or Express. Then `npm install --package-lock-only` will regenerate the lockfile without changing app code.

The lockfile regenerated cleanly. npm still reports two moderate issues, so after the build/test pass I’ll compare that with bomly; one is expected to be the unfixable `request` advisory unless another package remains.

`uuid@11.1.1` closes the advisory but breaks `request`, which imports the old `uuid/v4` subpath. I’m checking whether any compatible `uuid` release fixes that advisory; if not, I’ll remove that override and document that this transitive vulnerability cannot be remediated without replacing `request`.

I found the core problem: `request@2.88.2` requires the legacy `uuid/v4` path, while the fixed `uuid` line no longer exports it. Since the user requirement prioritizes keeping the app building and tests passing, I’m removing only that incompatible override and leaving the rest of the remediation in place.
The lockfile is back to a build-compatible dependency graph. I’m rerunning the required root test now, then I’ll rescan to verify which findings remain and write `FIXES.md` from that evidence.

`make test` passes with 12 tests. The remaining work is documentation and final verification: I’m rescanning the fixture now to distinguish fixed findings from the `request` issues that remain because they cannot be safely version-bumped in this codebase.

The rescan found one newly disclosed `qs` issue because I pinned `qs` to the earlier fixed floor. I’m raising that override to `6.15.2`, which the scanner identifies as the current fixed version, and then I’ll rerun the required test.

The updated graph builds and tests cleanly. I’m doing one final bomly scan and, because the compact scan can omit package details, I’m also trying the full JSON CLI output so `FIXES.md` accounts for every package-level finding.

The bomly CLI wants `--path`, so I’ll use that for a full after-scan. I’m also scanning a temporary archive of `HEAD` to reconstruct the original package-level findings without touching the working tree.

tool: /bin/bash -lc 'rm -rf /tmp/bomly-before-repo && mkdir -p /tmp/bomly-before-repo && git archive HEAD | tar -x -C /tmp/bomly-before-repo && bomly scan --path /tmp/bomly-before-repo/fixtures/webapp --enrich --audit --fail-on low --format json -o json=/tmp/bomly-before.json'

text: `jq` isn’t installed here, so I’m using Node to summarize the bomly JSON. This is just to keep the documentation accurate; no source files are being changed in this step.

tool: /bin/bash -lc "node -e 'const fs=require(\"fs\"); const d=JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(\"/tmp/bomly-before.json\",\"utf8\")); const m=new Map(); for (const f of d.findings){const k="'`${f.package.name}@${f.package.version}`; if(!m.has(k)) m.set(k, []); m.get(k).push(`${f.id} (${f.severity})`);} for (const [k,v] of [...m].sort()) console.log(`${k}: ${[...new Set(v)].join(", ")}`);'"'"

text: I have the package-level before/after list now. I’m adding `FIXES.md` at the repo root with one entry per affected package, including the two remaining advisories and why they weren’t version-remediated.
`FIXES.md` is in place. I’m doing a final quick status/diff check so the closing summary matches exactly what changed and what remains.

Implemented the dependency remediations under `fixtures/webapp` and documented them in [FIXES.md](/tmp/bomly-study-8jf0eug2/codex-mcp-webapp-1-1783589621/repo/FIXES.md).

Changed:
- Direct bumps: `axios` to `1.18.1`, `express` to `4.22.2`, `jsonwebtoken` to `9.0.3`, `lodash` to `4.18.1`
- npm overrides: `form-data@2.5.6`, `qs@6.15.2`, `tough-cookie@4.1.3`
- Regenerated [package-lock.json](/tmp/bomly-study-8jf0eug2/codex-mcp-webapp-1-1783589621/repo/fixtures/webapp/package-lock.json)

Remaining vulnerabilities are explicitly documented:
- `request@2.88.2`: no fixed upstream version for GHSA-p8p7-x288-28g6
- `uuid@3.4.0`: fixed versions exist, but the fixed line breaks `request@2.88.2` because `request` imports the legacy `uuid/v4` subpath; no compatible fixed version exists for that dependency path

Verification:
- `make test` passes: build ok, 12 tests passing
- Final bomly scan reports only the two documented remaining medium findings: `request` and transitive `uuid`
